blurb: Thick, tongue-shaped leaves speckled with pale warts, held in two ranks when young. One of the most shade-tolerant and forgiving succulents going — named for its stomach-shaped flowers (gaster = stomach).
size: Strap leaves ~4–6 in, offsetting into a clump

care.light: Bright indirect light to partial shade suits it best — it's one of the few succulents genuinely happy out of direct sun, which can scorch pale patches on the leaves.
care.water: Soak-and-dry, watering at the base to keep water out of the leaf crevices. Let it dry fully between drinks; less in winter.
care.temperature: Room temperature; keep above ~40°F, no frost.
care.dormancy: Opportunistic — grows in the milder months, rests in extreme heat and cold.
care.feeding: A dilute feed once or twice in the growing season.
care.potting: Slow and content snug; repot mainly to divide the clumping offsets.
care.propagation: Offsets are the easy route; leaf cuttings also work but are slow.
care.bloom: Arching stalks of curved, stomach-shaped pink-and-green flowers — the source of the name.
care.troubles: Overwatering rot and sunburn are the main issues; occasionally mealybugs.
care.toxicity: Generally kept as a pet-safe succulent — Gasteria isn't among the plants flagged as poisonous. It's absent from the ASPCA's list either way, so read this as “no known toxicity” rather than a positive all-clear.

sources.title: Gasteria gracilis — NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
